finding: When --delete/--delete-project/--clean refuse because opencode is running, the error is a single generic line ('Active opencode --continue process detected') regardless of how many are running and gives no detail to act on (no PID/count/CWD/TTY/start time).
proposed_action: Replace the returncode-only check with a process enumerator that lists each matching process (PID, TTY, CWD, start time/elapsed, count) in the error, so the user can identify and manage them.

finding: The pgrep check is copy-pasted in 3 sites (delete-session, delete-project, cleanup) with identical text; enhancing it in one place should update all.
proposed_action: Extract a single helper (e.g. detect_running_opencode() -> list[ProcInfo] and a formatter) used by all three call sites (KISS/DRY).

finding: pgrep -f 'opencode --continue' matches ANY process whose full command line contains that substring (e.g. an editor, a shell running a script, or ocman's own analysis) — not just real opencode sessions. This can both over-report and mislead.
proposed_action: In the new enumerator, filter to plausible opencode processes (e.g. argv0 basename == 'opencode' with a 'continue' arg), and exclude the current process/ancestors; document the heuristic.

finding: User would like each running opencode's session id. opencode does not expose the session id in process args/env in a documented, stable way; deriving it is uncertain and could be wrong.
proposed_action: Do NOT claim a session id per process (would be a guess). Best-effort: map each process CWD to a project worktree in the DB and show the project (labelled 'cwd -> project', best-effort). Session id itself deferred.

finding: 'When did it last do something' (CPU/IO activity) is not cheaply/reliably available; /proc CPU times exist but interpreting them as 'last activity' is fuzzy and platform-specific.
proposed_action: Report the cheap, honest signal instead: process START time + elapsed running time (ps lstart/etimes). Label it 'started', not 'last active'. True last-activity deferred.

finding: ps/proc details differ by platform; the request caps effort at ~2s (no deep recursion). Windows path already skips the check (sys.platform=='win32').
proposed_action: Implement with a single `ps` call (portable columns) + optional /proc/<pid>/cwd on Linux; degrade gracefully (show PIDs only) if ps output is unavailable; hard per-call timeout; no per-process forking loops beyond the cheap cwd readlink.

finding: The message mentions --force but a richer listing should keep the actionable guidance (how to bypass, and that closing the listed PIDs clears the check).
proposed_action: Keep/clarify the --force hint and add 'close the processes above, or re-run with --force' in the enriched message.
